dialogue_tree.title: Module 12: Anticipatory Coping & Relapse Prevention
dialogue_tree.learning_objective: Identify situations that might make change harder; Create flexible coping responses; Normalize lapses and focus on learning; Separate behavior from identity
dialogue_tree.description: Learn to prepare patients for challenges and normalize setbacks as part of the change process

nodes.patient_statement: I've been walking regularly for a few weeks now and it's going well. I feel good about it.
nodes.patient_context: Patient reports success with initial change

practitioner_choices.text: That's great! Just make sure you don't stop now. You have to keep it up.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This creates pressure without actually preparing for challenges.

practitioner_choices.text: That's really wonderful progress. As you continue, what situations might make it harder to keep walking?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Perfect - affirms success and proactively prepares for challenges without being pessimistic.

practitioner_choices.text: Good, but the hard part is maintenance. Most people fail at this stage.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This emphasizes failure rather than building preparation skills.

nodes.patient_statement: Oh, now I'm worried I'm going to fail too. Maybe I'm being too optimistic.

practitioner_choices.text: I didn't mean to worry you. Your success so far shows you're capable. What I meant was, what challenges do you think might come up as you continue?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Good recovery - affirms capability and reframes to constructive problem-solving.

practitioner_choices.text: You should be worried. Maintenance is when most people slip up. You need to be realistic.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This reinforces discouragement rather than building coping skills.

nodes.patient_statement: Well, the weather is getting colder. And the holidays are coming up - I'll be busier with family stuff. Those might make it harder.

practitioner_choices.text: You just can't let those things be excuses. You need to prioritize your health.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This labels legitimate challenges as 'excuses' rather than problem-solving.

practitioner_choices.text: Cold weather and busy holidays - those are real challenges many people face. What has helped you in the past when the weather was bad or you had a busy schedule?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent - normalizes challenges and explores patient's existing coping resources.

practitioner_choices.text: Don't let cold weather stop you. Just dress in layers and use willpower.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This minimizes the challenge without collaboratively problem-solving.

nodes.patient_statement: In the past when it was cold, I used to walk in the mall sometimes. And when I'm busy... honestly, I haven't figured that one out yet. I just let exercise slide.
nodes.patient_context: Patient identifies one solution and one gap

practitioner_choices.text: Mall walking is a great strategy you've used before. For busy times, if you find yourself with a packed schedule, what could you do to keep some movement in your day, even if it's not a full walk?
practitioner_choices.technique: Affirming past success + If-then coping question
practitioner_choices.feedback: Perfect - reinforces existing coping and creates implementation intention for the gap.

practitioner_choices.text: You can't let a busy schedule be an excuse. You have to make time no matter what.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This creates pressure and doesn't acknowledge that flexibility might be needed.

nodes.patient_statement: Maybe I could do shorter walks? Like even just 10 minutes, a couple times when I have a break? Something is better than nothing, right?

practitioner_choices.text: You're creating a flexible backup plan - if a full walk isn't possible, then doing shorter walks when you can. That's smart thinking. How would you feel about yourself if you did miss a day or two?
practitioner_choices.technique: Affirming flexible coping + Preparing for potential lapse
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent - reinforces the adaptive plan and proactively addresses potential self-judgment.

practitioner_choices.text: 10 minutes isn't really enough to make a difference. You should stick to the 30-minute walks every time.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This dismisses the patient's realistic backup plan and creates all-or-nothing thinking.

nodes.patient_statement: Honestly? I'd probably feel like I failed. That I'm not disciplined enough.

practitioner_choices.text: If you miss a day, you've failed. That's just the reality of making changes.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This reinforces unhelpful all-or-nothing thinking about setbacks.

practitioner_choices.text: Setbacks are a common part of change, not failure. Missing a day doesn't erase all the walking you've done. What would you want to tell a friend who missed a day of their walking plan?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Perfect - normalizes setbacks and uses perspective to challenge self-judgment.

nodes.patient_statement: I'd tell them it's okay, that they should just start again the next day. I guess I'm harder on myself than I'd be on someone else.

practitioner_choices.text: You'd be compassionate with a friend. What would it be like to extend that same kindness to yourself if you miss a day?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Good - highlights the insight and invites applying it to self.

practitioner_choices.text: Well, you should be hard on yourself. That's what creates accountability.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This contradicts the helpful insight the patient just had.

nodes.patient_statement: That would be... different. I think it would help me get back on track faster instead of spiraling into feeling bad about myself.

practitioner_choices.text: So if you do miss a day or two during the holidays, what might help you get back to walking?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Perfect - creates a specific plan for recovery from setback.

practitioner_choices.text: Good, but try not to miss any days. Just stay disciplined.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This undoes the helpful work on normalizing and planning for setbacks.

nodes.patient_statement: I think reminding myself that I've built this habit - I can get back to it. Maybe looking at my walking log to see all the days I did walk. And then just going for a short walk to restart, not waiting until I can do the 'full' walk. Just getting back into motion.
nodes.patient_context: Successful anticipatory coping and relapse prevention
nodes.outcome: Good engagement - Through anticipatory coping and normalizing setbacks, the patient developed flexible strategies and a compassionate approach to challenges.
nodes.learning_summary: Anticipatory coping involves proactively identifying challenges and planning flexible responses. Relapse prevention work includes: normalizing setbacks as part of change, separating behavior from identity ('I missed a walk' not 'I'm a failure'), and creating specific recovery plans. This builds resilience and maintains motivation through the inevitable challenges of behavior change.

nodes.patient_statement: I feel like if I slip up even once, I'll have failed. This all feels like a lot of pressure now.
nodes.outcome: Poor engagement - Creating rigid expectations and treating lapses as failure increased pressure and decreased resilience.
nodes.learning_summary: Creating rigid all-or-nothing expectations, treating lapses as failures, reinforcing harsh self-judgment, and pressuring perfect adherence all undermine resilience. Effective relapse prevention requires flexibility, self-compassion, and recognition that setbacks are normal opportunities for learning rather than evidence of failure.
